skill.name: voice-ai-engine-development
skill.description: Build real-time conversational AI voice engines with async worker pipelines, streaming transcription, LLM agents, text-to-speech synthesis, interrupt handling, and multi-provider patterns.
skill.summary: Guides implementation of real-time voice AI systems using async Python, WebSockets, streaming STT, LLM agents, and TTS.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but most are false positives from markdown code fences, silent audio byte literals, and documentation links. The remaining concern is moderate: runnable examples and templates demonstrate network services and API-key configuration without showing production authentication or secret-management controls.

medium_findings.title: Unauthenticated WebSocket Example Binds to All Interfaces
medium_findings.description: The runnable example accepts a WebSocket connection, processes incoming audio bytes, and starts Uvicorn on 0.0.0.0. This is useful for a demo, but a copied deployment would expose a voice conversation endpoint without documented authentication, origin checks, or rate limits.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly accepts WebSocket clients and binds to all interfaces. The risk is moderate because this is an example application, not hidden behavior in the skill runtime.

medium_findings.title: Provider API Key Templates Require Secret Controls
medium_findings.description: The templates and provider guide include configuration fields for provider API keys and credential JSON paths. No credential exfiltration was found, but users should store secrets outside source control and avoid logging provider configuration.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The key and credential fields are explicit and relevant to real provider integrations. They appear to be placeholders and configuration examples, so the concern is handling risk rather than malicious access.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The external command detections come from markdown code fences and inline formatting in documentation. No evidence found that these backticks execute Ruby, shell commands, or user-controlled commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations are markdown examples or formatting, not executable Ruby or shell syntax. A repository search did not find subprocess, os.system, eval, or exec usage.

low_findings.title: Silent Audio Byte Literals Misclassified as Obfuscation
low_findings.description: Hex byte sequences are used to create silent PCM audio chunks for transcription muting and mock synthesis. No evidence found that these bytes hide executable code, encoded payloads, or evasive behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The bytes are simple null-byte audio samples used in documented audio-processing examples. They are not decoded, executed, or transmitted as hidden commands.

low_findings.title: Documentation Links Misclassified as Network Behavior
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs in the README are links to provider documentation. They do not make outbound requests from the skill and do not send local data to external services.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are plain markdown resource links. There is no code path that performs an HTTP request at those locations.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography and Network Reconnaissance Detections Were Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The flagged README lines describe voice AI concepts and testing topics, not cryptographic algorithms or reconnaissance tooling. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, port scanning, or host discovery code in the reviewed context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged text is prose about system capabilities and testing. The static labels do not match the observed content at the cited lines.

dangerous_patterns.title: Open WebSocket Service Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The example server accepts WebSocket audio input and is started on all interfaces. Use authentication, origin validation, TLS, request size limits, and rate limits before production use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The network service pattern is directly visible in the example. It is dangerous mainly if copied into production unchanged.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Provider Secret Configuration Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The factory template passes provider API keys and credential references through configuration objects. This is expected for provider integrations but must be paired with secret storage and redaction.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The lines explicitly reference API key fields. They are commented template placeholders, so the finding is moderate rather than high.

content.value_statement: Real-time voice AI requires careful coordination between audio streaming, transcription, language models, and speech synthesis. This skill provides async Python patterns, examples, and provider guidance for building interruptible conversational voice systems.
actual_capabilities: Explains async queue-based worker pipelines for transcriber, agent, synthesizer, and audio output components.
Shows how to handle user interrupts during bot speech and preserve partial conversation state.
Provides example patterns for streaming transcription, buffered LLM responses, and text-to-speech output.
Compares common STT, LLM, and TTS providers for cost, latency, quality, and deployment fit.
Includes templates for multi-provider factories and reusable worker classes.
Documents common implementation pitfalls such as echo loops, audio jumping, memory leaks, and latency.
limitations: Examples are instructional and require production security controls before deployment.
Provider integrations are templates or mock implementations, not complete SDK wrappers.
The skill does not supply hosted infrastructure, credentials, or provider accounts.
Audio quality and latency still depend on network conditions, codec choices, and provider performance.

use_cases.title: Prototype a Voice Assistant
use_cases.description: Use the worker pipeline and WebSocket example to build a first version of a real-time voice assistant.

use_cases.title: Compare Voice AI Providers
use_cases.description: Review provider tradeoffs for transcription, LLM response generation, and text-to-speech synthesis.

use_cases.title: Fix Streaming Conversation Bugs
use_cases.description: Use the pitfalls guide to diagnose echo, skipped audio, slow responses, and interrupt failures.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Basic Voice Bot
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me design a simple real-time voice assistant using this skill. Include the worker components, data flow, and minimal provider choices.

prompt_templates.title: Add Interrupt Handling
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my current voice pipeline and explain how to add user interrupts during bot speech while preserving accurate transcript history.

prompt_templates.title: Choose Providers for Production
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare transcription, LLM, and TTS providers for a production customer support voice agent with low latency and moderate cost.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Voice Engine Design
prompt_templates.prompt: Evaluate my voice AI architecture for latency, backpressure, error handling, cleanup, credential handling, and WebSocket security gaps.

output_examples.input: I need a voice assistant that supports interruptions and natural turn taking.
output: A pipeline design with separate transcriber, agent, synthesizer, and output workers.
A description of mute and unmute behavior to prevent echo during bot speech.
A plan for tracking partial bot messages when the user interrupts playback.

output_examples.input: My bot audio jumps between sentences during streaming playback.
output: A likely cause analysis focused on sentence-by-sentence synthesis.
A recommendation to buffer the LLM response before sending it to the synthesizer.
Checks for queue timing, chunk pacing, and cleanup of stale audio tasks.

output_examples.input: Which providers should I use for a low-cost multilingual voice app?
output: A provider comparison across transcription, LLM, and TTS layers.
A suggested combination optimized for language coverage and operating cost.
Notes about credentials, regions, latency testing, and fallback providers.

best_practices: Add authentication, origin checks, TLS, request limits, and rate limits before exposing any WebSocket endpoint.
Store provider credentials in a secret manager or environment-specific configuration outside source control.
Test latency, interrupt behavior, cleanup, and provider failure paths with realistic audio streams.
anti_patterns: Do not deploy the example server unchanged on a public interface.
Do not log API keys, credential paths, raw audio, or complete transcripts without a privacy policy.
Do not stream every partial LLM token directly to TTS when stable speech playback is required.

faq.question: Does this skill build a complete hosted voice platform?
faq.answer: No. It provides implementation guidance, examples, and templates for building your own voice AI engine.

faq.question: Which programming language does it focus on?
faq.answer: The examples focus on async Python patterns with queues, WebSockets, and streaming workers.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The marketplace metadata marks support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Does it include real provider credentials?
faq.answer: No. Credential entries are placeholders and must be supplied securely by the user.

faq.question: Is the example server production ready?
faq.answer: No. Add authentication, TLS, origin validation, monitoring, and rate limits before production use.

faq.question: What problems does the pitfalls guide address?
faq.answer: It covers audio jumping, echo loops, failed interrupts, memory leaks, lost context, latency, and poor audio quality.
